text.title: AI Failure Mode Taxonomy Failure Modes
text.summary: Five recurring failure modes of taxonomies themselves with symptom / root-cause / fix.

antipattern.description: Open list — new modes added on every postmortem; list grows to 80+.
antipattern.symptom: No team remembers the list; ids stop being used; reports use ad-hoc strings again.
antipattern.root-cause: No closed-vocabulary discipline; "completeness" valued over usability.
antipattern.fix: Cap at 12. Adding a 13th requires retiring an existing mode (rule r1).

antipattern.description: No detectors — modes defined by prose only.
antipattern.symptom: Two engineers tag the same incident differently; eval pass-rates are not comparable across runs.
antipattern.root-cause: Definition-only entries never tested in practice.
antipattern.fix: Every mode must have a concrete detector (machine signal or human checklist) (rule r2).

antipattern.description: Severity-uniform — every mode is "high"; severity column is useless.
antipattern.symptom: Alert channel floods; on-call paged on cosmetic format drift.
antipattern.root-cause: Severity was assigned by mode author without business-impact review.
antipattern.fix: Severities calibrated against business impact (revenue, safety, customer trust); reviewed quarterly.

antipattern.description: No link to mitigations — taxonomy is a dictionary, not a routing table.
antipattern.symptom: Mode fires, on-call asks "now what?", playbook nowhere; same incident replayed.
antipattern.root-cause: Taxonomy treated as documentation, not operational asset.
antipattern.fix: Every mode links to ≥1 mitigation/eval methodology (rule r5).

antipattern.description: Retire-and-forget — modes are dropped without deprecation, breaking dashboards.
antipattern.symptom: Dashboards show NULL columns where retired ids used to live; queries silently fail.
antipattern.root-cause: Retirement treated as housekeeping, not a versioned event.
antipattern.fix: Quarterly deprecation cadence: 1 quarter both-accepted, then removed (rule r4).
